trajectory_summary: The call built genuine warmth through a shared-humanity middle stretch — bill pain confirmed, vulnerability disclosed, pitbull rapport — but Brandon never converted that warmth into a secure appointment. The booking is tentative in the literal sense: no computer requirement stated, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, the 'we'll let you know' wrap-up leaves the appointment soft. The call ends with Tanya cooperative but not committed.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag 'tentative_appointment / qualified' is directionally correct but optimistic on quality. The appointment was set without the button-up anchors (no-computer requirement, 'is that fair' handshake, time-blocked framing) that predict show rate. Combined with an unresolved trust pattern around prior solar no-shows, this appointment carries meaningful ghost risk. A more precise tag would be 'fragile_tentative_appointment / qualified.'

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Vulnerability disclosure: acknowledge and bridge, don't continue checklist
primary_coaching_focus.quote: That that lost me my job, and so now I'm living off of Social Security, which is not a whole lot. So I gotta make every penny count these days.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Tanya handed Brandon the emotional foundation for the entire pitch — fixed income, job loss, 'make every penny count' — and he responded by asking about the kWh graph. That same material resurfaced as a payment-confusion objection at 15:26 and required two more exchanges to partially resolve. One sentence of acknowledgment at 05:18 that connected Tanya's situation to solar's value proposition would have done the work preemptively. Walk Brandon through what a brief acknowledgment-and-bridge looks like at that moment so he can see the connection between the missed emotional beat and the objection handling he had to do later.

key_moments.stewart_read: Tanya is on a landline and has already said she cannot send a photo from this phone. Brandon has been working toward a photo send for roughly two minutes at this point. The bill is in Tanya's hand — she's reading numbers aloud — but Brandon continues to ask for a photo rather than pivoting to a verbal readback protocol. The landline context was apparent by 05:54 ('Yeah. But not on this phone. Hang on.'). Strategic read: Brandon may have been hoping Tanya could switch to her cell. Reactive read: Brandon didn't pivot because he doesn't have a clean verbal-readback protocol ready. The reactive read holds — the photo path continued for another five-plus minutes burning time on an impossible channel, and the bill data ultimately captured (13,000 kWh annual, 1,500 kWh March) was retrieved verbally anyway.

key_moments.quote: Okay. Here, I'm back. Okay. Great. It's hard for me to get around. I have a sclerotic nerve problem. That that lost me my job, and so now I'm living off of Social Security, which is not a whole lot. So I gotta make every penny count these days.
key_moments.stewart_read: Tanya discloses sciatica, job loss, and Social Security income in a single unprompted statement. This is a trust gift — she's telling Brandon her real stakes and her real constraints. Brandon's response ('Just right there, it should have a graph') moves directly back to bill collection without any acknowledgment of the weight of what she just said. The empathy beat required here is not a long detour — a single sentence connecting her financial situation to the value of solar as a fixed-expense reduction would have served both the emotional and the commercial purpose.

key_moments.quote: How much do I have to put down? Could you tell me about the other time?
key_moments.stewart_read: Tanya asks the down-payment question immediately after the appointment is nominally set — this is the first financial objection after commitment. For a Social Security customer who just said she has to make every penny count, this is not a casual question. Brandon answers with 'nothing down' correctly, but then drifts into a mid-pitch explanation of how the program works rather than connecting 'no money down' directly back to Tanya's stated financial situation. The flip was available here: 'Nothing down — and the goal is that what you're paying Ion replaces what you're paying Potomac Edison, so you're not adding a new expense, you're redirecting one you already have.' Brandon's response was accurate but missed the emotional anchor point. V1 tagged this as 'partial' — the Stage 3 read explains why: correct content, wrong emotional register for this customer.

Brandon asks 'Was there anyone else on the title of the home?' and Tanya confirms it's just her. Brandon moves on. Taken in isolation this looks clean. But by 05:18, Tanya has disclosed she is on Social Security with a physical disability, living alone. The combination of fixed income, health constraints, and a significant financial decision suggests an adult child or family member may be informally involved in her decisions even if not on the title. Brandon never probed. This is not a hard protocol violation — Tanya said she's the sole owner — but a soft probe ('Is there anyone you'd want on the call with you when we go over the design?') would have surfaced any informal decision-maker and prevented a last-minute 'let me ask my daughter' cancellation. The risk is proportionate to Tanya's vulnerability profile.
